briefings.headline: Former nun recounts 12 years inside a Carmelite monastery in new memoir
briefings.body: Catherine Coldstream entered a Carmelite monastery at age 24 and spent 12 years living a cloistered life of prayer and obedience before leaving. She has written about the experience in her memoir, Cloistered: My Years as a Nun, offering a firsthand account of what life inside such a community could look like for those who commit to it.

sources.title: A former nun explains why she ran away from her 'Cloistered' life

briefings.why_it_matters: Firsthand accounts from people who have left religious institutions could offer rare insight into cloistered communities that are otherwise closed to public view.

briefings.headline: West Virginia's paid relocation program is turning temporary remote workers into permanent residents
briefings.body: West Virginia's Ascend program, launched during COVID, offered cash incentives to attract digital nomads to the state. The initiative is one of dozens of similar relocation programs across the country designed to give economically struggling areas a boost. The program appears to be achieving its goals, with a notable share of participants choosing to stay long-term rather than moving on.

sources.title: West Virginia paid digital nomads to move there during COVID. Many are staying

briefings.why_it_matters: Relocation incentive programs could expand to more states, meaning remote workers may increasingly find themselves eligible for cash payments simply for choosing where to live and work.

briefings.headline: Justice Dept. drops New York Times subpoenas after judicial pushback
briefings.body: The Justice Department has withdrawn subpoenas seeking grand jury testimony and phone records from New York Times journalists. The decision came after a federal judge sharply questioned the government's pursuit of the records during a Thursday hearing. The move ends an effort to compel journalists to reveal information that could have exposed confidential sources.

sources.title: Justice Dept. drops 'New York Times' subpoenas under pressure from judge

briefings.why_it_matters: When the government withdraws subpoenas targeting journalists under court pressure, it could signal limits on how far federal investigators can go in compelling reporters to disclose their sources.

briefings.headline: Musk Says 'Let's Enjoy the Ride' as AI Fears Grow
briefings.body: Elon Musk, speaking to The Economist, said he is unconcerned about public criticism and denied being a racist. The interview comes amid mounting public anxiety over artificial intelligence development. Musk, a prominent figure in both the tech and AI industries, appeared to downplay concerns, framing the current moment as one to embrace rather than fear.

sources.title: 'Let's enjoy the ride' says Elon Musk, as AI fears mount

briefings.why_it_matters: Musk's views on AI carry weight given his influence over major technology platforms and companies, meaning his public stance could shape how AI risks are discussed and regulated.

briefings.headline: Schools face a new literacy frontier: teaching students to write
briefings.body: As reading instruction reforms gain traction, educators are turning attention to a parallel gap: writing. At Sand Creek Elementary in Highlands Ranch, Colorado, first grade teachers are using structured writing lessons to build composition skills early, with some teachers reporting stronger student growth than in previous years. Researchers and educators increasingly argue that writing instruction has long been underfunded and under-prioritized in American classrooms.

sources.title: The next literacy challenge: Teaching students how to write

briefings.why_it_matters: Students who leave school without strong writing skills could face lasting disadvantages in higher education and the workforce, where clear written communication is a core requirement across nearly every field.

briefings.headline: Rayner Rules Out Rent Controls in England
briefings.body: Housing Secretary Angela Rayner has told the BBC that rent controls will not be introduced in England, citing the Renters Rights Act as already having a significant impact on the housing market. Rayner indicated the government believes existing legislation is sufficient to address tenant concerns, closing the door on a policy some housing advocates had pushed for as a direct check on rising rental costs.

sources.title: Angela Rayner rules out rent controls in England

briefings.why_it_matters: Renters hoping for government caps on how much landlords can charge will need to look to existing protections under the Renters Rights Act rather than any new price controls, which could limit their options if rents continue to rise.

quick_hits.text: Brent crude surged over 6% to hit $100 per barrel Thursday for the first time since May, driven by escalating Middle East conflict.

source.title: Oil prices hit $100 for the first time since May

quick_hits.text: Google has been fined $1 billion by the EU under the Digital Markets Act, becoming the third tech giant penalized under the landmark law.

source.title: Google hit with $1 billion in fines as EU braces for Trump battle

quick_hits.text: The Justice Department withdrew subpoenas targeting New York Times reporters, with lead newsroom lawyer David McCraw speaking publicly about the reversal.

source.title: New York Times lawyer talks about DOJ withdrawing subpoenas sent to Times' reporters

quick_hits.text: A historic cyclospora outbreak is devastating Michigan, leaving some residents using the bathroom up to 20 times daily.

source.title: Using a bathroom 20 times a day - life in the middle of US cyclospora outbreak

quick_hits.text: University of Chicago mathematician Yu Deng won a Fields Medal for solving equations that predict fluid behavior from its smallest components.

source.title: Yu Deng Predicts the Actions of the Whole From Its Tiniest Parts

voice_blocks.title: This Day
voice_blocks.text: 2025: Angara Airlines Flight 2311 crashes on approach to Tynda Airport, killing all 48 people on board.

closer.text: "A popular government without popular information, or the means of acquiring it, is but a prologue to a farce or a tragedy, or perhaps both."
closer.attribution: James Madison, letter to W.T. Barry (1822)

source.title: James Madison, letter to W.T. Barry (1822)
